scenes.title: Two Objects, Two Temperatures
scenes.visualIntent: Open with matched metal and wooden spoons, equal thermometer readings and different hand-sensation indicators; separate temperature, transfer and conduction labels.

scenes.title: Temperature Is A Measurement
scenes.visualIntent: Demonstrate a digital thermometer settling in cool and warm tap water; show Celsius units and a safe-temperature boundary without displaying boiling vessels.

checkpoint.answer: A temperature reading, usually recorded in degrees Celsius.

scenes.title: Heat Transfer Has A Direction
scenes.visualIntent: Place a cool metal spoon into warm tap water, chart both temperature trends and draw a labelled energy-transfer arrow from water to spoon.

checkpoint.prompt: Which way does energy transfer when a cool spoon enters warm water?
checkpoint.answer: From the warmer water towards the cooler spoon.

scenes.title: Contact Makes A Path
scenes.visualIntent: Trace a conduction pathway along a metal spoon using adjacent vibrating regions rather than travelling particles; include a stop-if-hot safety cue.

scenes.title: Metal And Wood
scenes.visualIntent: Show a controlled spoon comparison with matched dimensions, immersion depth and timer; animate a faster temperature rise along metal than wood.

checkpoint.prompt: Why does the metal handle usually warm faster than the wooden handle?
checkpoint.answer: Metal is a better thermal conductor, so energy transfers through it faster.

scenes.title: The Particle Story
scenes.visualIntent: Use a same-colour lattice with a motion gradient that evens out; overlay a model-limit note about scale, count and omitted metal-electron detail.

checkpoint.prompt: Do particles travel from the warm end to the cool end during conduction in a solid?
checkpoint.answer: No. Energy transfers through interactions while particles stay in their overall positions.

scenes.title: Cooling Is Transfer Too
scenes.visualIntent: Chart warm water cooling towards room temperature and separate contact conduction arrows from muted air-movement and evaporation pathways labelled beyond today's focus.

scenes.title: Meeting In The Middle
scenes.visualIntent: Animate two thermometer traces converging for a sealed cool-water bottle in warm tap water; keep particles moving after the traces meet.

scenes.title: Equilibrium Is Not Instant
scenes.visualIntent: Run a time-lapse graph with steep early changes and flatter later changes; reveal factors affecting the final reading without introducing equations.

scenes.title: Feeling Can Mislead
scenes.visualIntent: Compare hand-to-metal and hand-to-wood transfer rates at equal measured temperature; highlight faster skin cooling beside metal, with a no-touch-unknowns symbol.

scenes.title: Everyday Insulation
scenes.visualIntent: Show wrapped and unwrapped matched cups with slow and fast transfer arrows; connect the same insulation principle to warm and cool food storage.

scenes.title: Repair The Heat Claims
scenes.visualIntent: Correct four misconception cards using measured-temperature, direction, rate and equilibrium tiles; finish with conduction arrows pointing up, down and sideways.

scenes.title: Choose The Best Material
scenes.visualIntent: Present a practical lunch-container choice with temperature-over-time curves; include an adult food-safety boundary and no absolute-temperature promise.

scenes.title: Plan A Contact Investigation
scenes.visualIntent: Build a stepwise fair-test plan around two spoons, adult-prepared warm water, fixed depth, timer and thermometer; show excluded hazardous equipment.

scenes.title: Tell The Whole Transfer Story
scenes.visualIntent: Complete a four-step transfer storyboard for spoon and cold-bottle examples, then append a model-limit footer and a final temperature-versus-process contrast.

checkpoint.prompt: What four ideas belong in a complete contact heat-transfer explanation?
checkpoint.answer: Temperature comparison, contact pathway, transfer direction, and the temperature trend towards equilibrium.
